title: Cat Scratch on Eyeball: Immediate Emergency Guide
meta_desc: A cat scratch on the eye is a high-urgency veterinary emergency. Learn about corneal injuries and why immediate professional care is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: A corneal injury is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent rapid infection, intense pain, and permanent vision loss.

What is a corneal injury and how does it affect my pet's eye?

  • A scratch directly on the eyeball is known medically as a corneal ulcer or a corneal laceration.
  • The cornea is the clear, outer layer of the eye that acts as a sensitive protective window.
  • Because it is thin, even a minor scratch from a cat's claw can penetrate the layers, causing intense pain and risking the structural integrity of the globe.

Is a corneal scratch or eye injury considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-urgency emergency because eye injuries are unpredictable and can worsen within hours.
  • A scratch can introduce bacteria deep into the eye, leading to rapid infection, "melting" of the cornea, or permanent blindness.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian or a veterinary ophthalmologist immediately.

How does providing a photo help the veterinarian triage my pet?

  • Providing a clear, high-resolution photo to your vet during your initial call can be life-saving.
  • A photo allows the triage team to see if there is a perforation (a hole through the eye), an aqueous flare (inflammation inside the eye), or a visible flap of tissue.
  • This helps the medical team prepare the necessary equipment and prioritize your pet the moment you walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Penetrating intraocular injuries, such as those caused by cat claws, are ophthalmic emergencies requiring rapid diagnosis and aggressive therapy to maintain vision. These injuries may result in corneal defects, intraocular hemorrhage, lens perforation (leading to rapid cataract formation if the anterior lens capsule tear is >2 mm), vitreal and retinal hemorrhage, and retinal detachments. Secondary complications can include corneal scar formation, septic endophthalmitis, cataract formation, and secondary glaucoma. Ophthalmic ultrasonography and orbital radiology are helpful to assess the integrity of intraocular tissues.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1697)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my pet?
faqs.answer: No. Never use human medications or over-the-counter eye drops on a scratched eyeball. Some ingredients, like steroids, can cause the cornea to dissolve if an ulcer is present.

faqs.question: How can I protect the eye until I get to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your pet immediately to prevent them from pawing or rubbing the eye, which can turn a minor scratch into a rupture.

faqs.question: What are the signs that the scratch is severe?
faqs.answer: Signs of high severity include the eye looking cloudy or blue, visible blood inside the eye, a change in the pupil shape, or the pet being unable to open the eye at all.
